title: The Little Prince
author: After Saint-Exupéry
paragraphs: Once, when I was six years old, I saw a magnificent picture in a book about the jungle. It showed a boa constrictor swallowing a wild beast whole. The book said that boa constrictors swallow their prey without chewing, and then they sleep for six months while they digest. I thought about this for a long time, turning the pages slowly in the lamplight, and then I made my very first drawing. It was a picture of a boa constrictor that had swallowed an elephant. The shape was smooth and rounded, like a great lumpy hat sitting quietly on the page. I was very proud of it. I thought it was rather terrifying.

I showed my masterpiece to the grown-ups and asked whether my drawing frightened them. They looked at it and said, why should anyone be frightened of a hat? My drawing was not a picture of a hat, of course. It was a picture of a boa constrictor digesting an elephant. So I drew another picture, this time showing the inside, so the grown-ups could understand. I drew the elephant sitting neatly inside the snake's belly, its legs tucked beneath it, looking quite comfortable. But the grown-ups told me to put away my drawings of boa constrictors, whether from the inside or the outside, and devote myself instead to geography, history, arithmetic, and grammar.

That is how, at the age of six, I gave up what might have been a magnificent career as a painter. I had been discouraged by the failure of my Drawing Number One and my Drawing Number Two. Grown-ups never understand anything by themselves, and it is tiresome for children to be always and forever explaining things to them. So I chose another profession and learned to fly airplanes. I flew all over the world, and geography was indeed quite useful. At a glance I could tell China from Arizona, which is very helpful if you happen to get lost in the night. I had many serious encounters with many serious people throughout my life, but none of them ever truly understood me.

Whenever I met a grown-up who seemed a little clearer-headed than the rest, I would show them my Drawing Number One, which I had always kept. I wanted to see if they truly understood. But each time, the response was the same. That is a hat, they said. And so I would never talk to them about boa constrictors, or primeval forests, or stars. I would lower myself to their level and talk about bridge and golf and politics and neckties. The grown-up would be delighted to have met such a sensible person. And I would live my life alone, with no one I could really talk to, until the day my engine failed over the Sahara Desert.

It happened six years ago. Something in my engine had broken, and since I had neither a mechanic nor passengers with me, I set out to try to make the repair all alone. It was a matter of life and death. I had scarcely enough drinking water to last a week. The first night I went to sleep on the sand, a thousand miles from any human habitation. I was more isolated than a shipwrecked sailor on a raft in the middle of the ocean. So you can imagine my amazement, at sunrise, when I was awakened by an odd little voice. It said, please, if you would be so kind, draw me a sheep.

I jumped to my feet as though I had been struck by lightning. I blinked my eyes hard. I looked carefully all around me. And I saw a most extraordinary small person, who stood there examining me with great seriousness. He did not appear to be lost, or dying of thirst, or frightened. He did not look at all like a child stranded in the middle of the desert, a thousand miles from anywhere. When I managed to speak at last, I said, but what are you doing here? And he repeated, very softly, very gently, very patiently, if you please, draw me a sheep. There was such authority in his request that I obeyed.

The trouble was, I had never drawn a sheep. So I drew for him one of the only two pictures I knew how to make, the one of the boa constrictor from the outside. I was astounded to hear the little fellow answer, no, no, no! I do not want an elephant inside a boa constrictor. A boa constrictor is a very dangerous creature, and an elephant is very cumbersome. Where I live, everything is very small. What I need is a sheep. Draw me a sheep. No grown-up had ever understood my Drawing Number One before. This small stranger in the desert saw through it immediately. I picked up my pencil again, deeply curious.

I drew a sheep. He looked at it closely and said, no, that one is already very sickly. Make me another. So I drew again. My friend smiled gently and said with patience, you see yourself that this is not a sheep. It is a ram. It has horns. I drew yet another sheep, but it was rejected too. This one is too old, he said. I want a sheep that will live a long time. By this time my patience was wearing thin, because I was very eager to start taking my engine apart. So I scribbled a drawing of a box, with three holes in it. Your sheep is inside, I told him. I was surprised to see a light of understanding on the face of my young judge.

That is exactly the kind of sheep I wanted, he exclaimed. Do you think this sheep will need a great deal of grass? Why do you ask, I said. Because where I live, everything is very small. There will surely be enough grass, I said. It is a very small sheep. He bent his head over the drawing. Not so small that, he murmured. He has gone to sleep. And that is how I made the acquaintance of the little prince. It took me a long time to learn where he came from. He would ask me many questions, but whenever I asked him one, he seemed not to hear. It was words dropped by chance that, little by little, revealed everything.

When he saw my airplane for the first time, he asked, what is that thing? It is not a thing, I said. It flies. It is an airplane. It is my airplane. I was proud to tell him I could fly. He cried out, then you fell from the sky! Yes, I said modestly. He laughed his extraordinary golden laugh, and that annoyed me considerably. I like my misfortunes to be taken seriously. Then you, too, come from the sky, he said. Which planet are you from? I caught a gleam of light in the mystery of his presence, and I pressed him. So you come from another planet? But he did not answer. He simply gazed at my airplane with a gentle, thoughtful look.

Little by little, as the days passed, I learned about his planet. The little prince came from a tiny asteroid, hardly any bigger than a house. I believe it was Asteroid B-612. This asteroid was first spotted through a telescope in 1909 by a Turkish astronomer, who presented his discovery at an International Astronomical Congress. But nobody believed him because he was wearing Turkish costume. Grown-ups are like that. Fortunately for the reputation of Asteroid B-612, the Turkish astronomer put on a European suit and made his demonstration again. This time everybody accepted his report. I have told you these details because of the grown-ups. They love numbers and credentials.

When you tell a grown-up about a new friend, they never ask, what does his voice sound like? What games does he love best? Does he collect butterflies? Instead they ask, how old is he? How many brothers does he have? How much does his father earn? Only from these figures do they think they know him. If you were to say to the grown-ups, I saw a beautiful house made of rosy brick with geraniums in the windows and doves on the roof, they would not be able to picture it. You must say, I saw a house worth a hundred thousand dollars. Then they exclaim, oh, how pretty! That is just how they are. One must not hold it against them.

On the little prince's planet, as on all planets, there were good plants and bad plants. And therefore there were good seeds from good plants and bad seeds from bad plants. But seeds are invisible. They sleep deep in the heart of the earth's darkness until one of them is seized with the desire to awaken. Then it stretches itself and begins to push a timid little sprig upward toward the sun. If it is a sprig of radish or rose, one can let it grow wherever it likes. But when it is a bad plant, one must pull it up right away, as soon as it can be recognized. On the little prince's planet, there were terrible seeds called baobabs.

The soil of the planet was infested with baobab seeds. If you attend to a baobab too late, you can never get rid of it. It spreads over the entire planet, its roots piercing right through. And if the planet is too small, and the baobabs are too many, they split it to pieces. It is a question of discipline, the little prince told me later. When you have finished washing and dressing each morning, you must attend to your planet. You must pull up the baobabs regularly, as soon as you can tell them apart from the rosebushes, which they resemble when very young. It is very tedious work, he said, but very easy, if you do it faithfully every day.

One day the little prince suggested I make a beautiful drawing of baobabs, to warn the children back on Earth. Children, he said, if you ever travel near a small asteroid, beware of baobabs! I worked hard over that drawing. I colored the baobabs with the sense of urgency that the occasion demanded, great and terrible trees whose roots could crack a world in two. The lesson was serious. Laziness and carelessness lead to baobabs. A bit of neglect, and a tiny sprout becomes an unstoppable giant. Look after your world each morning, tend the small things before they become big things. This is a lesson not only for asteroids, but perhaps for all of us who struggle with the seeds in our own hearts.

I learned that the little prince's life had been very lonely. For a long time his only entertainment had been watching sunsets. On his tiny planet, all he had to do was move his chair a few steps, and he could watch the twilight whenever he wished. One day, I found out, he had seen the sunset forty-four times. You know, he said, when one is so terribly sad, one loves sunsets. Were you so terribly sad on that day of the forty-four sunsets, I asked. But the little prince did not answer. He looked away toward where the horizon would be, and a distant softness settled into his eyes. Some feelings are too deep and too tender to be spoken aloud.

I came to understand that the sunsets were tied to the loneliness in his heart, to the ache of tending a small world all by himself, day after day. There was something missing from his life, though he could not name it yet. The sky would turn amber and gold and violet, and the little prince would sit very still, watching the light dissolve. Each sunset was a kind of lullaby, a brief moment of beauty before the darkness arrived. He watched them the way one watches a candle in a window, hoping someone might see the light and come closer. But on Asteroid B-612, there was no one else to share the evening. Only the silent turning of the stars.

Then one day, a seed arrived on his planet from nobody knows where. Perhaps the wind carried it from some distant corner of the universe. A sprout appeared, unlike any the little prince had ever seen before. He watched it carefully, suspicious at first that it might be a new kind of baobab. But the bush soon stopped growing and began to produce a flower. The little prince watched the bud swell day after day, preparing itself behind its green chamber, choosing its colors with great care, adjusting its petals one by one. It did not wish to come out all wrinkled like a poppy. It wanted to appear only in the full radiance of its beauty.

And then one morning, exactly at sunrise, the flower revealed herself. She yawned and stretched her petals open and said, ah, I am scarcely awake. You must forgive me. My petals are still all disarranged. The little prince could not help admiring her. How beautiful you are, he said. Am I not, she replied sweetly. And I was born at the same moment as the sun. The little prince could see quite well that she was not especially modest, but she was so lovely. She had arrived with the dawn, her petals glowing like new silk, and the whole tiny planet smelled of her perfume. His heart stirred in a way it never had before.

But the flower was complicated. She began to torment the little prince with her vanity almost at once. One day, speaking of her four thorns, she said to him, let the tigers come with their claws! The little prince protested, there are no tigers on my planet, and besides, tigers do not eat plants. I am not a plant, the flower answered softly. And she added, I am not at all afraid of tigers, but I have a horror of drafts. You would not happen to have a screen? A horror of drafts, the little prince thought, that is bad luck for a plant. This flower is a very complex creature. She would cough and demand to be sheltered, and he would feel guilty.

In the evening, she would ask to be put under a glass globe. It is very cold where you live, she told him, though she had come from a seed that had traveled across the universe. She could not have known what the climate was like. The truth was, she used her coughs and her complaints to make the little prince feel tender toward her, to keep him close. She was playing little games with him, games of love that he was too young to understand. She loved him very much in her own thorny, roundabout way, but her pride would not allow her to show it plainly. And so, between the little prince and his rose, there grew a quiet misunderstanding.

The little prince told me all this with sadness in his voice. I ought not to have listened to her, he said. One must never listen to the flowers. One should simply look at them and breathe their fragrance. My flower filled my whole planet with her perfume, but I could not enjoy it. The story of the thorns and tigers, which annoyed me so, should have moved me to tenderness. He sighed. I was too young to know how to love her. I should have judged her by her deeds and not her words. She cast her fragrance and her radiance over me. I should never have run away. I should have guessed the affection behind her poor little tricks. But I was too young.

Still, the morning came when the little prince decided to leave. He cleaned out his two active volcanoes, which were very useful for heating his breakfast. He also cleaned the extinct volcano, because you never know. He pulled up the last of the baobab shoots. And then, with a heavy heart, he watered his rose for the last time. As he placed the glass globe over her, he found that he wanted to cry. Goodbye, he said. The flower coughed. But it was not because she had a cold. I have been silly, she said at last. I ask your forgiveness. Try to be happy. She did not want him to see her cry. She was a very proud flower.

Go, she said to him. I want you to go. She let the globe stand near, but she did not ask him to lower it over her just yet. She wanted to feel the open air. I love you, she murmured, though the words came out almost too softly to hear. The little prince was very surprised. It is my fault, she went on. There is no point in our both being foolish. But try to be happy. Leave the glass dome. I do not want it anymore. But the wind, he protested. I am not so very susceptible to cold. The night air will do me good. As for the tigers, I have my thorns. And she showed him her four thorns, simply and bravely.

The little prince set off on his journey through the cosmos, traveling by means of a flock of wild birds. He visited six small planets before coming to Earth, and each planet was inhabited by a single grown-up. The first planet was home to a king. He sat regally on a magnificent throne of purple and ermine. When he saw the little prince, he cried out, ah, here is a subject! The king believed he ruled over everything, the stars, the sun, the wind. But he was a reasonable king, and he gave only reasonable orders. If I ordered a general to turn into a seagull, he explained, and the general did not obey, it would not be the general's fault. It would be mine.

The king offered to make the little prince a minister of justice. But there is no one here to judge, said the little prince, looking around the tiny planet. Then you must judge yourself, replied the king gravely. That is the most difficult thing of all. It is much harder to judge yourself than to judge others. If you succeed in judging yourself rightly, then you are indeed a man of true wisdom. The little prince thought the king was not unreasonable, but the planet was very small and there was nothing to reign over. He asked permission to leave. The king, not wishing to give an order that would not be obeyed, quickly appointed him ambassador, and the little prince slipped away into the stars.

The second planet was inhabited by a conceited man. When he saw the little prince approaching, he cried out from far away, ah, a visit from an admirer! For to conceited people, all other people are admirers. He wore a magnificent hat and asked the little prince to clap his hands. When the little prince clapped, the conceited man raised his hat in a modest salute. This was more entertaining than the visit to the king, the little prince thought, at least for a minute or two. He clapped again, and the hat went up again. After five minutes of this, the little prince grew tired of the monotony. He asked, what must one do to make the hat go down?

But the conceited man did not hear him. Conceited people never hear anything but praise. Do you really admire me very much, he asked. What does it mean to admire, asked the little prince. To admire means that you regard me as the handsomest, the best-dressed, the richest, and the most intelligent person on the planet. But you are the only person on your planet, said the little prince. Admire me anyway, please. Just to make me happy. The little prince shrugged his small shoulders. I admire you, he said, but what is there in that to interest you so? And he moved along. Grown-ups are certainly very peculiar, he thought to himself as he traveled onward through the deep and quiet dark.

The third planet was the home of a drunkard. The little prince found him sitting silently before a collection of empty bottles and a collection of full bottles. What are you doing, asked the little prince. He felt a great pity rising within him. I am drinking, said the drunkard mournfully. Why are you drinking, asked the little prince. To forget, replied the drunkard, lowering his head. To forget what, pressed the little prince, who was already beginning to feel sorry for him. To forget that I am ashamed, the drunkard confessed, sinking lower in his chair. His eyes would not meet the little prince's.

Ashamed of what, the little prince asked, hoping he might be able to help. Ashamed of drinking, answered the drunkard, and he shut himself up in a great silence. The little prince walked away, bewildered and deeply sad. Grown-ups are certainly very, very odd, he said to himself as he continued on his journey through the cold spaces between the stars. The drunkard's world had been the saddest of all, a tiny planet of circular regret, where forgetting required the very thing that created the shame. The little prince carried that heaviness with him for a while, like a stone in his pocket, before the stars gently lightened his step again.

The fourth planet belonged to a businessman. This man was so busy that he did not even lift his head when the little prince arrived. Three and two make five. Five and seven make twelve. Twelve and three make fifteen. Good morning. Fifteen and seven make twenty-two. He had been counting for fifty-four years, and he had never been interrupted except three times, once by a beetle, once by an attack of rheumatism, and once by this little prince. Twenty-six and five make thirty-one. He was counting the stars. The little prince was very confused. He waited patiently for a pause in the arithmetic.

When the businessman finally looked up, the little prince asked him what he did with the stars. I own them, said the businessman firmly. I manage them. I count them and recount them. It is difficult work. It is a matter of consequence. He wrote the number of stars on a small slip of paper. Then he locked the paper in a drawer. And that is all, he said. The little prince was not satisfied. I own a flower which I water every day. I own three volcanoes which I clean out every week. It is of some use to my volcanoes and to my flower that I own them. But you are of no use to the stars. The businessman opened his mouth to reply, but found no answer.

The little prince left the fourth planet with a clearer sense of what it meant to truly possess something. You do not own a thing by counting it or locking it in a drawer. You own a thing by caring for it, by giving your time and your heart to it. The stars did not need the businessman, and the businessman did not truly need the stars. He only needed the idea of owning them, which is not the same thing at all. The little prince thought about his flower, alone under her glass globe, and he felt something tighten in his chest. She needed him. That was what mattered. That was what made her his.

The fifth planet was very curious. It was the smallest of all the planets he had visited, and there was just barely room on it for a street lamp and a lamplighter. The little prince could not understand what use a street lamp and a lamplighter could be, there in the sky, on a planet that had no people and no houses. Yet he said to himself, it is possible that this man is absurd. But he is not as absurd as the king, the conceited man, the businessman, or the drunkard. At least his work has meaning. When he lights his lamp, it is as if he brought one more star to life, or one flower. When he puts it out, it sends the star or the flower to sleep.

The lamplighter was in a terrible predicament. His planet used to turn at a reasonable pace, and he would light the lamp in the evening and put it out in the morning, having the rest of the day for resting and the rest of the night for sleeping. But the planet's rotation had sped up over the years. Now a full day passed in only one minute, and the lamplighter had no rest at all. He lit the lamp. He put it out. He lit it again. He put it out again. His forehead glistened with perspiration. Good morning, he would say, and good evening, all in the same breath. The little prince watched him with great tenderness.

The little prince admired the lamplighter deeply. He is the only one of them all who does not seem ridiculous to me, he thought. Perhaps that is because he is thinking of something else besides himself. He is faithful to his orders, even when they have become impossible. The little prince wished he could stay and be his friend. The planet was so small, though, that there was no room for two. And the lamplighter could not stop, not even for a moment. He would have liked to help him somehow, to give him rest. But there was nothing to be done. The little prince sighed and moved on, carrying with him a new admiration for those who serve faithfully, even when no one is watching.

The sixth planet was ten times larger than the last. It was inhabited by an old gentleman who wrote voluminous books. He was a geographer. A geographer is a scholar who knows the location of all the seas, rivers, cities, mountains, and deserts. His planet was magnificent, and he looked quite important sitting behind his great desk. When he saw the little prince, he said, here is an explorer! The little prince sat down on the desk, out of breath from so much traveling. Where do you come from, asked the old gentleman. What is your planet like? Is it large? The little prince answered all his questions and then asked, what about your planet? It is very beautiful.

Have you any oceans, the little prince asked. I could not tell you, said the geographer. Have you any mountains? I could not tell you, said the geographer. Have you any towns or rivers or deserts? I could not tell you, replied the geographer once more. But you are a geographer! said the little prince, astonished. Exactly, the old man said. I am a geographer, not an explorer. A geographer does not go out to count the cities, the rivers, the mountains, the seas, the oceans, and the deserts. The geographer is much too important to go loafing about. He does not leave his desk. But he receives the explorers in his study and writes down their accounts.

The little prince told the geographer about his planet, about his volcanoes and his flower. We do not record flowers, said the geographer firmly. Why not, asked the little prince. Flowers are the most beautiful things on my planet. Because flowers are ephemeral, said the geographer. What does ephemeral mean? asked the little prince, who had never in his life let go of a question once he had asked it. It means that which is in danger of speedy disappearance. Is my flower in danger of speedy disappearance? asked the little prince. Certainly it is, the geographer replied. My flower is ephemeral, the little prince repeated to himself, and she has only four thorns to defend herself against the world.

For the first time, the little prince felt a sharp pang of regret for having left his rose behind. She was all alone on his tiny planet with only her four small thorns. The geographer's cold, scholarly word, ephemeral, had pierced something in his heart. His flower could vanish. His flower could wilt. His flower could die while he wandered the universe visiting ridiculous grown-ups. He wanted suddenly to go back, but his journey carried him forward. The geographer, seeing the little prince's distress, recommended that he visit the planet Earth. It has a good reputation, he said. And so the little prince set off for the seventh planet, which was Earth, the largest and most complicated of them all.

Earth is not just any planet. On Earth there are one hundred and eleven kings, including, to be sure, the African kings. There are seven thousand geographers, nine hundred thousand businessmen, seven and a half million drunkards, and three hundred and eleven million conceited people. That is to say, about two billion grown-ups. To give you an idea of the size of the Earth, before the invention of electricity, it was necessary to maintain a whole army of lamplighters across all six continents. Seen from a distance, it would have been a splendid sight, all those lamps being lit in sequence, like the steps of a ballet, rolling across the surface of the globe.

The little prince landed on Earth, in the Sahara Desert, in Africa. He looked around but saw nobody. He was beginning to be afraid he had come to the wrong planet when a coil of gold, the color of moonlight, stirred in the sand. Good evening, said the little prince, courteously. Good evening, said the snake. What planet is this, asked the little prince. This is the Earth. This is Africa, said the snake. The little prince looked at the snake with curiosity. The snake was thin, like a finger, and yet there was something powerful and ancient about it. It wound itself around the little prince's ankle like a golden bracelet. The night was still, and the sand was cool.

The snake spoke in riddles, as snakes do. Whom you touch, I send back to the earth from which they came, said the snake. But you are innocent, and you come from a star. I feel sorry for you, so weak on this hard earth of granite. I can help you someday, if you grow too homesick. I can send you back. The little prince understood perfectly what the snake was talking about. You speak in riddles, he said. I solve them all, said the snake. And they both fell silent, there in the moonlight, in the endless quiet of the desert. The little prince looked up at the stars and wondered which one was his, where his flower was waiting, alone under her glass globe.

The little prince crossed the desert and came at last to a high mountain. He had only ever known mountains that reached up to his knees, his three little volcanoes on Asteroid B-612. He used the extinct one as a footstool. A mountain as tall as this one, he thought, will let me see the entire planet at one glance, and all the people on it. But all he saw, from the top of the mountain, were sharply pointed crags of rock stretching endlessly in every direction. The Earth seemed dry and angular and forbidding. Hello, he called out. Hello, hello, hello, answered the echo, bouncing off the stone walls of the canyons below.

Who are you, asked the little prince. Who are you, who are you, who are you, the echo replied. Be my friends. I am all alone, he said. I am all alone, I am all alone, I am all alone, repeated the echo. What a peculiar planet, the little prince thought to himself. It is entirely dry and pointed and salty. And the people have no imagination. They repeat whatever one says to them. On my planet I had a flower, and she always spoke first. Her voice was warm and sometimes cross, but it was always her own. Here the mountain could only give back what it received. The little prince descended the slope, feeling the loneliness of the vast, empty Earth pressing in on every side.

He walked for a long time through sand and rocks and snow, and at last he came upon a road. All roads lead to the habitations of men, the little prince reflected. He followed the road and came to a garden. It was a garden in full bloom, and in it were five thousand roses, all of them open and radiant and fragrant. They looked exactly like his flower. Good morning, said the roses. The little prince stared at them in dismay. He felt a sudden tightness in his throat. His flower had told him that she was the only one of her kind in the whole universe. And here were five thousand of her kind, all in one single garden.

She would be very much annoyed if she saw this, the little prince thought. She would cough tremendously and pretend to be dying to avoid being laughed at. And I would have to pretend to nurse her back to health, for if I did not, she would really let herself wilt, just to humiliate me. Then he said to himself, I thought I was rich with a flower that was unique in all the world, and all I had was a common rose. That and my three volcanoes, which come up to my knees, one of which may be extinct forever. That does not make me a very great prince. And he lay down in the grass and wept.

He wept among the roses for a long time, his face pressed into the cool green grass, feeling smaller and more lost than he had ever felt before. All his pride in his special flower, all his belief that she made him rich, seemed to dissolve like morning dew. The universe was enormous and indifferent, and his rose was just one of thousands. The petals around him nodded gently in the breeze, oblivious to his sorrow. The sun moved across the sky, and the shadows of the rose bushes shifted and lengthened. The little prince did not move. He simply lay there, listening to the wind in the petals, feeling the ache of something precious slipping away from him.

It was then that the fox appeared. He came trotting out from beneath an apple tree, his copper fur gleaming in the late afternoon light. Good morning, said the fox. Good morning, said the little prince politely, though he was feeling quite miserable. He looked around but could not see anyone. I am right here, said the fox, under the apple tree. Come and play with me, said the little prince. I am so unhappy. I cannot play with you, said the fox. I am not tamed. The little prince considered this. What does that mean, to tame? It is an act too often neglected, said the fox. It means to establish ties.

To me, the fox continued, you are nothing more than a little boy who is just like a hundred thousand other little boys. And I have no need of you. And you, on your part, have no need of me. To you I am nothing more than a fox like a hundred thousand other foxes. But if you tame me, then we shall need each other. To me, you will be unique in all the world. To you, I will be unique in all the world. The little prince began to understand. I am beginning to think of something, he said slowly. There is a flower. I think she has tamed me. Quite possibly, said the fox. On Earth one sees all sorts of things.

The fox looked at the golden wheat fields stretching away in the distance. I do not eat bread, he said. Wheat is of no use to me. The wheat fields have nothing to say to me. And that is sad. But you have hair that is the color of gold. Think how wonderful that will be when you have tamed me! The grain, which is also golden, will bring me back the thought of you. And I shall love the sound of the wind in the wheat. The little prince sat quietly, thinking about golden things, about his hair, about wheat, about the warmth of sunlight. The fox waited patiently, his bright eyes shining. Please, tame me, he said at last.

What must I do, asked the little prince. You must be very patient, replied the fox. First you will sit down at a little distance from me, like that, in the grass. I shall look at you out of the corner of my eye, and you will say nothing. Words are the source of misunderstandings. But each day you may sit a little closer. The next day the little prince came back. It would have been better to come back at the same hour, said the fox. If you come at four in the afternoon, then by three I shall begin to be happy. I shall feel happier and happier as the hour advances. By four I shall be excited and worried. I shall discover the price of happiness.

And so the little prince tamed the fox. They met every day at the same hour. Every day they sat a little closer. The fox's heart beat faster each afternoon as the appointed time drew near. The little prince learned the fox's habits, the way he liked to sit near the vineyard, the way his ears pricked up at small sounds. And the fox learned the little prince's laugh, the way he tilted his head when he was thinking, the way his golden hair caught the light. They were taming each other, though neither of them quite realized it. The world was becoming richer for both of them, one golden thread of connection at a time.

When the day came for the little prince to leave, the fox sat very still and said, ah, I shall cry. Then it has done you no good at all to be tamed, said the little prince. It has done me good, said the fox, because of the color of the wheat fields. The little prince looked at him sadly. Go look at the roses again, the fox said. You will understand now that your rose is unique in all the world. Then come back to say goodbye to me, and I will make you a present of a secret. The little prince walked slowly back to the rose garden, turning the fox's words over in his mind.

He stood before the five thousand roses and addressed them quietly. You are not at all like my rose, he said. As yet you are nothing. No one has tamed you, and you have tamed no one. You are like my fox when I first knew him. He was only a fox like a hundred thousand other foxes. But I have made him my friend, and now he is unique in all the world. The roses were very much embarrassed. They had no answer for this small boy who spoke so gently and so firmly. You are beautiful but you are empty, he continued. One could not die for you. Ordinary passersby would think my rose looked just like you. But in herself alone she is more important than all of you together.

She is more important, he went on, because it is she whom I have watered. Because it is she whom I have placed under a glass globe. Because it is she whom I have sheltered behind a screen. Because it is she whose caterpillars I have killed, except the two or three that we saved to become butterflies. Because it is she whom I have listened to, when she grumbled, or boasted, or even sometimes when she said nothing at all. Because she is my rose. The words settled over the garden like warm rain. The five thousand roses bowed their heads slightly in the breeze. The little prince turned away from them and walked back through the tall grass to where the fox was waiting.

The fox was sitting exactly where the little prince had left him, his tail curled around his paws. So now here is my secret, said the fox. It is very simple. One sees clearly only with the heart. What is essential is invisible to the eye. What is essential is invisible to the eye, the little prince repeated, so that he would be sure to remember. It is the time you have wasted for your rose that makes your rose so important. It is the time I have wasted for my rose, murmured the little prince, tasting each word carefully, letting it sink deep into his being. The fox nodded, his amber eyes shining with quiet knowledge.

People have forgotten this truth, said the fox. But you must not forget it. You become responsible, forever, for what you have tamed. You are responsible for your rose. I am responsible for my rose, the little prince repeated softly. He said the words like a vow, like a prayer whispered before sleep. The fox smiled his gentle fox smile, and the little prince bent down and kissed him on the forehead. Then he walked away, carrying the fox's secret in his heart like a lantern. The world looked different now. The wheat fields were golden. The sky was tender. Even the stones on the road seemed to hold some hidden warmth. Everything was connected by invisible threads of care.

After leaving the fox, the little prince continued his journey across the Earth. He came upon a railway switchman who sorted out the travelers, sending them to the right in express trains and to the left in express trains, in great bundles of a thousand at a time. An express train roared past, its windows bright, its passengers pressed against the glass. Where are all those people going, asked the little prince. Not even the locomotive engineer knows that, said the switchman. Another express thundered past in the opposite direction. Are they coming back already, asked the little prince. Those are not the same ones, said the switchman. It is an exchange.

The people are never satisfied where they are, said the switchman. They rush away and rush back, always seeking something new, never finding it in the place where they stand. Only the children know what they are looking for, said the little prince thoughtfully. They spend their time on a rag doll and the doll becomes very important to them. And if somebody takes the doll away from them, they cry. The switchman nodded gravely. They are lucky, he agreed. The little prince watched another train disappear into the distance, its lights fading like dying stars. He thought of his own small planet, and how he had not known to be happy there. He understood that lesson now.

Then the little prince met a merchant who sold pills that had been invented to quench thirst. You just swallow one pill a week, and you have no need at all of anything to drink. Why do you sell those, asked the little prince. Because they save a great deal of time, said the merchant. Computations have been made by experts. With these pills, you save fifty-three minutes in every week. And what does one do with those fifty-three minutes? One does whatever one likes, said the merchant, waving his hand vaguely. The little prince said to himself, if I had fifty-three minutes to spend as I liked, I should walk very slowly toward a spring of fresh water.

The little prince thought about the merchant for a long time after he left. He thought about how grown-ups were always in such a terrible hurry, rushing from one place to the next, swallowing pills and counting minutes, and yet they never seemed to know what they were hurrying toward. A glass of cool water, drawn slowly from a deep well, savored sip by sip in the shade of a tree, was worth more than all their saved-up minutes. Happiness was not a destination you could reach by going faster. It was something you found by being fully present, by sitting in the grass and watching the light change, by listening to the silence between the words.

By now I had been stranded in the desert for eight days. I had listened to the story of the merchant as I drank the last drop of my water supply. My hands were dry and my lips were cracked. I said to the little prince, your stories are very charming, but I have not yet repaired my airplane and I have nothing left to drink. I am beginning to fear that I may die of thirst. It is a good thing to have had a friend, even if you are about to die, the little prince said. I, for instance, am very glad to have had a fox for a friend. He did not seem to understand the danger at all.

I told him quite seriously that we needed to find water. He agreed. It is good to search for water, he said dreamily. Let us walk. And so we set out together, the two of us, into the vast emptiness of the Sahara under the stars. We walked for hours in silence. Night fell, and the stars came out, and the sand shimmered like silver in the moonlight. I carried the little prince when he grew sleepy. He was so light, no heavier than a bundle of feathers. I held him in my arms and walked on through the cool dark. His eyes were closed and his lips moved slightly, as if he were still talking to his rose in a dream.

What makes the desert beautiful, the little prince murmured, half asleep, is that somewhere it hides a well. I was surprised. I had always loved the desert too, though I had never known why. In the desert you sit down on a sand dune. You see nothing. You hear nothing. And yet something shines in the silence. The desert is beautiful because of what it conceals, because of the promise hidden beneath the surface. I looked down at the little prince, his face pale and peaceful in the starlight. What moved me so deeply about this sleeping child was his loyalty to a flower, the image of a rose that shone through his whole being like the flame inside a lamp.

At daybreak we found the well. It was not like the wells of the Sahara, which are merely holes dug in the sand. It was like a real village well, with a rope and a pulley and a bucket. The pulley moaned like an old weathervane that the wind has long since forgotten. It sang as I drew the water up. The little prince laughed. Do you hear, he said, we have wakened the well, and it is singing. I did not want him to tire himself. Let me do the work, I said. I lifted the heavy bucket to the edge of the well and set it there steadily. The water shone in the morning light like liquid gold.

I held the bucket to the little prince's lips, and he drank with his eyes closed, as one might receive a sacrament. The water was like nothing else. It was more than just water. It was born of the walk under the stars, the song of the pulley, the effort of my arms. It was good for the heart, like a gift on a winter morning. When I was a small boy, the little prince said, the lights of the Christmas tree, the music of the midnight mass, the tenderness of smiling faces, all those things made up the radiance of the Christmas gift. This water was like that. It was not merely water. It was sweetness born of effort and starlight.

The little prince said something that stayed with me. On your planet, people grow five thousand roses in a single garden, and they do not find what they are looking for. And yet what they are looking for could be found in a single rose, or a little water. He was right. The eyes are blind. You have to look with the heart. I drank the water and breathed deeply. The sand at dawn was the color of honey. I was happy in that honey-colored light, but I also felt a quiet dread. The little prince had a faraway look, as though he were already planning his return to the stars. Something was about to end, and I could feel it in my bones.

The little prince told me he had been on Earth for nearly a year. The anniversary of his landing was approaching. He had come down very close to this spot. I felt a strange uneasiness. Then it was no accident, I said, that eight days ago you were walking all alone, a thousand miles from any inhabited region. You were returning to the place where you landed. The little prince blushed. And I added hesitantly, perhaps it was because of the anniversary? The little prince blushed once more. He never answered questions. But when one blushes, it means yes, does it not? There was something he was not telling me, something heavy and soft at the same time.

I had finished repairing my engine. I went back to tell the little prince the good news. But his face was pale and his expression grave. He asked me, you remember, you promised me a muzzle for my sheep? You promised me a strap for the muzzle? You are responsible for your sheep. I drew the muzzle and handed it to him. My heart ached with a sorrow I could not explain. What are you preparing for, I asked him. He did not answer directly. Tonight, he said softly, it will be one year since I arrived. My star will be directly above the place where I fell to Earth last year. There was something final in his voice that frightened me.

Do not come tonight, the little prince said gently. I will look as if I am very ill. I will look a little as if I am dying. It is like that. Do not come to see that. It is not worth the trouble. I will not leave you, I said. But the little prince's face was serious. I tell you this because of the snake, he said. He must not bite you. Snakes, they are malicious creatures. They can bite for the pleasure of it. But I will not leave you, I repeated. Then a thought seemed to reassure him. It is true that the snake has no venom left for a second bite. And he smiled, a small brave smile, and looked up at the sky.

That evening, the little prince slipped away without my noticing. When I caught up with him, he was walking quickly and with great determination toward a particular spot in the desert. He spoke to me calmly and tenderly. You understand, he said. It is too far. I cannot take this body with me. It is too heavy. I felt as though something were squeezing my heart very tightly. But it will be only an old abandoned shell, he continued. There is nothing sad about old shells. He sat down gently in the sand because he was frightened, though he tried not to show it. You know, he said, it will be very nice. I too shall look at the stars.

All the stars will be wells with a rusty pulley, he said, and every star will pour out fresh water for me to drink. That will be so amusing! You will have five hundred million little bells, and I shall have five hundred million springs of fresh water. He tried to laugh, but the laugh caught in his throat. You see, he said, it is too far. I cannot take this body with me. Do not be sad, he whispered. He hesitated for a moment, then he rose to his feet and took one step forward. The little prince stood very still in the moonlight. There was nothing but a flash of yellow, close to his ankle, like a bracelet. He fell as gently as a tree falls.

There was not even any sound, because of the sand. He fell softly, slowly, the way a leaf lets go of a branch. His body made no mark on the ground, so quiet was his departure. I stood there in the dark, unable to move, listening to the silence rushing in like water to fill the space where his voice had been. The desert was enormous and still. The stars above were very bright. I knew he had gone back to his planet, back to his rose and his three volcanoes. But the absence was so sudden, so complete, that it felt as though the whole desert had taken a deep breath and forgotten to let it out.

At sunrise, when I returned to the spot where the little prince had been, his body was gone. It was not so very heavy. The sand was smooth and unmarked, as if no one had ever stood there. I knew then that he had made it home. He was somewhere far above me, on his tiny asteroid, watering his rose in the morning light, pulling up the baobab sprouts, watching the sunset by moving his chair a few steps. The thought was a comfort, thin and bright as a thread of spider silk, but it was enough. He had gone back to the beginning, to the place where he was needed, where his love could do its quiet, daily work.

And now, many years have passed since then. I have never told this story before. My companions who saw me return were glad to see me alive. I was sad, but I told them it was just exhaustion. I have healed, little by little, though not entirely. I know he has gone back to his planet because at sunrise there was no body on the sand. He was not so heavy. And at night I love to listen to the stars. They sound like five hundred million little bells. All the bells are laughing, and I laugh with them, standing alone in the garden, my face turned up to the dark sky. My friends think I am mad. Let them think so.

But sometimes my laughter turns to tears, and I think, has the sheep eaten the rose? The little prince would never forget to fasten the muzzle, of course. But what if one night he forgot to put the glass globe over her? What if the sheep got out of its box while he was sleeping? Then all the bells in the sky would suddenly stop, and all five hundred million stars would weep. It is a great mystery. For those of you who love the little prince, as I do, nothing in the universe can be the same if somewhere, on a star we cannot see, a sheep that we do not know has, yes or no, eaten a rose. Look up at the sky and ask yourself.

Has the sheep eaten the flower, or has it not? And you will see how everything changes. No grown-up will ever understand that this is a matter of so much importance. But for us who understand life, the question matters more than any number, more than any calculation, more than all the geography in the world. The little prince's rose is either blooming or gone, and the answer makes the whole sky different. That is how love works. That is what the fox taught us, there beneath the apple tree, in the golden light of afternoon. What is essential is invisible to the eye, but the heart sees it, holds it, carries it like a lantern through the dark.

To me, this is the loveliest and saddest landscape in the world. It is a stretch of sand in the Sahara, where a small boy once appeared, spoke of roses and foxes and stars, and then vanished. If you ever travel in Africa and come upon this spot, do not hurry past. Wait there a little, directly under the stars. If a small person with golden hair appears, if he laughs, if he refuses to answer your questions, you will know who he is. Be kind to him. Do not let him be so sad. Send me word at once that he has come back. But for now, this story is over, and the night is here, and the stars are already beginning to sing.

Close your eyes now, dear listener, and imagine the little prince sitting on his asteroid, watching the sunset, his rose beside him under the clear glass dome. The evening wind carries the faintest trace of her perfume, and the stars are beginning to appear, one by one. Somewhere, a fox is watching the wheat fields turn gold. Somewhere, a lamplighter is faithfully tending his small flame. And somewhere in the desert, the well is waiting with its rusty pulley, ready to sing again at dawn. What is essential is invisible to the eye, but the heart knows it is there, always, patient and warm and unbreakable. You are loved. Rest now. Let the stars laugh softly for you, like five hundred million tiny bells. Goodnight.
